text: The surgeon begins dissection by creating a plane below the inferior mesenteric vein, proceeding along the mesocolic fascia from medial to lateral. The rectal mesentery is tented up, and the peritoneum is scored below the inferior mesenteric artery. Dissection continues below the artery, baring its root, which is clipped and divided. The medial to lateral dissection progresses along the mesocolic fascia to the lateral peritoneal attachment. The dissection extends into the pelvis, completing posterior dissection to the pelvic floor. The lateral peritoneal cut is made to mobilize the descending colon.

The surgeon scores the peritoneum over the iliac vessels to begin the pelvic node dissection. The ureters are dissected and medialized, while the vesico-hypogastric fascia is defined and preserved. Nodal tissue is dissected along the external iliac vessels, followed by dissection along the internal iliac vessels.

The surgeon identifies and divides the distal end of the obturator artery. The obturator nerve and vein are exposed, with the distal end of the obturator vein being divided. Careful dissection of nodal tissue along the obturator nerve is performed to avoid nerve injury. The obliterated umbilical artery is clipped and divided, followed by the division of the superior vesical artery. Complete dissection of remaining nodal tissue along the internal iliac vessels is achieved.

The surgeon identifies, clips, and divides the distal end of the obliterated umbilical artery. The nodal tissue along the obturator nerve is cleared, and the obturator vein is clipped and divided at its origin. Dissection proceeds along the internal iliac vessels to the lumbosacral trunk. The internal pudendal vessels are clipped and divided. The sacrospinous ligament is identified as a landmark for the ischial spine. Lateral dissection of the rectum continues while preserving the vasico-hypogastric fascia and hypogastric nerve. The middle rectal vessels are encountered, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon continues dissection along the internal iliac vessels, clearing nodal tissue along the internal pudendal vessels, which are clipped and divided. An anterior peritoneal cut is made, preserving the seminal vesicles. Due to nodal involvement at the ischial spine, the sacrospinous ligament is cut at its origin from the ischial spine. The right levator ani muscle is also cut, maintaining adequate soft tissue margins.

The surgeon uses an endo stapler to divide the specimen proximally. The remaining surgery is completed perineally, and the specimen is excised through the perineum. An arrow highlights the attachment to the sacrospinous ligament.
